section.title: Abimelech
section.sectionRationale: Gideon refused the crown, but his son Abimelech seizes it by blood. Bankrolled from a pagan temple, he murders his seventy brothers on one stone and is crowned at Shechem. Only Jotham escapes, and from Mount Gerizim he tells the fable of the trees who could find no worthy king and settled for a bramble, then curses Abimelech and Shechem to destroy one another. God sends a spirit of animosity, Gaal's revolt flares, Shechem falls and is sown with salt, its tower is burned, and Abimelech dies ingloriously when a woman drops a millstone on his head. It is one movement: self-appointed kingship gone monstrous, and God repaying evil.
section.thesis: Abimelech grasps by murder the kingship his father declined, and God, honoring Jotham's curse, turns the conspirators against one another so that Shechem and its self-made king consume each other in fire and blood, showing that kingship seized for self is a bramble that burns those it shades, and that a rescued people who will not have the LORD as King reap ruin, deepening the cry for the righteous King to come.
section.frameNote: Eight beats: Abimelech murders his brothers and is crowned, verses 1 to 6; Jotham's fable of the trees, verses 7 to 15; Jotham's warning and curse, verses 16 to 21; God sends a spirit of animosity, verses 22 to 25; Gaal's boast and revolt, verses 26 to 29; the fall of Shechem, verses 30 to 41; the city destroyed and the tower burned, verses 42 to 49; and Abimelech's death at Thebez, verses 50 to 57.

units.label: Abimelech murders his brothers and is crowned

verses.text: Now {{abimelech|Abimelech}} son of Jerubbaal went to his mother’s brothers at Shechem and said to them and to all the clan of his mother,
“Please ask all the leaders of Shechem, ‘Is it better for you that seventy men, all the sons of Jerubbaal, rule over you, or just one man?’ Remember that I am your own flesh and blood.”
And when his mother’s brothers spoke all these words about him in the presence of all the leaders of Shechem, their hearts were inclined to follow Abimelech, for they said, “He is our brother.”
So they gave him seventy shekels of silver from the temple of Baal-berith, with which Abimelech hired some worthless and reckless men to follow him.
He went to his father’s house in Ophrah, and on one stone murdered his seventy brothers, the sons of Jerubbaal. But Jotham, the youngest son of Jerubbaal, survived, because he hid himself.
Then all the leaders of Shechem and Beth-millo gathered beside the oak at the pillar in Shechem and proceeded to make Abimelech their {{melek|king}}.
When this was reported to Jotham, he climbed to the top of Mount Gerizim, raised his voice, and cried out: “Listen to me, O leaders of Shechem, and may God listen to you.
One day the trees set out to anoint a king for themselves. They said to the olive tree, ‘Reign over us.’
But the olive tree replied, ‘Should I stop giving my oil that honors both God and man, to hold sway over the trees?’
Then the trees said to the fig tree, ‘Come and reign over us.’
But the fig tree replied, ‘Should I stop giving my sweetness and my good fruit, to hold sway over the trees?’
Then the trees said to the grapevine, ‘Come and reign over us.’
But the grapevine replied, ‘Should I stop giving my wine that cheers both God and man, to hold sway over the trees?’
Finally all the trees said to the {{atad|thornbush}}, ‘Come and reign over us.’
But the thornbush replied, ‘If you really are anointing me as king over you, come and find refuge in my shade. But if not, may fire come out of the thornbush and consume the cedars of Lebanon.’

units.label: Jotham's warning and curse
units.note: may fire come out and consume

verses.text: Now if you have acted faithfully and honestly in making Abimelech king, if you have done well by Jerubbaal and his family, and if you have done to him as he deserves,
for my father fought for you and risked his life to deliver you from the hand of Midian,

but you have risen up against my father’s house this day and killed his seventy sons on a single stone, and you have made Abimelech, the son of his maidservant, king over the leaders of Shechem because he is your brother,
if you have acted faithfully and honestly toward Jerubbaal and his house this day, then may you rejoice in Abimelech, and he in you.
But if not, may fire come from Abimelech and consume the leaders of Shechem and Beth-millo, and may fire come from the leaders of Shechem and Beth-millo and consume Abimelech.”
Then Jotham ran away, escaping to Beer, and he lived there for fear of his brother Abimelech.

units.label: God sends a spirit of animosity
units.note: that the crime might come to justice

verses.text: After Abimelech had reigned over Israel for three years,
God sent a spirit of animosity between Abimelech and the leaders of Shechem and caused them to treat Abimelech deceitfully,

in order that the crime against the seventy sons of Jerubbaal might come to justice and their blood be avenged on their brother Abimelech and on the leaders of Shechem, who had helped him murder his brothers.
The leaders of Shechem set up an ambush against Abimelech on the hilltops, and they robbed all who passed by them on the road. So this was reported to Abimelech.

units.label: Gaal's boast and revolt
units.note: who is Abimelech that we should serve him

verses.text: Meanwhile, Gaal son of Ebed came with his brothers and crossed into Shechem, and the leaders of Shechem put their confidence in him.

And after they had gone out into the fields, gathered grapes from their vineyards, and trodden them, they held a festival and went into the house of their god; and as they ate and drank, they cursed Abimelech.

Then Gaal son of Ebed said, “Who is Abimelech, and who is Shechem, that we should serve him? Is he not the son of Jerubbaal, and is not Zebul his officer? You are to serve the men of Hamor, the father of Shechem. Why should we serve Abimelech?
If only this people were under my authority, I would remove Abimelech; I would say to him, ‘Muster your army and come out!’”
When Zebul the governor of the city heard the words of Gaal son of Ebed, he burned with anger.
So he covertly sent messengers to Abimelech to say, “Look, Gaal son of Ebed and his brothers have come to Shechem and are stirring up the city against you.
Now then, tonight you and the people with you are to come and lie in wait in the fields.
And in the morning at sunrise, get up and advance against the city. When Gaal and his men come out against you, do to them whatever you are able.”
So Abimelech and all his troops set out by night and lay in wait against Shechem in four companies.
Now Gaal son of Ebed went out and stood at the entrance of the city gate just as Abimelech and his men came out from their hiding places.
When Gaal saw the people, he said to Zebul, “Look, people are coming down from the mountains!” But Zebul replied, “The shadows of the mountains look like men to you.”
Then Gaal spoke up again, “Look, people are coming down from the center of the land, and one company is coming by way of the Diviners’ Oak.”
“Where is your gloating now?” Zebul replied. “You said, ‘Who is Abimelech that we should serve him?’ Are these not the people you ridiculed? Go out now and fight them!”
So Gaal went out before the leaders of Shechem and fought against Abimelech,
but Abimelech pursued him, and Gaal fled before him. And many Shechemites fell wounded all the way to the entrance of the gate.
Abimelech stayed in Arumah, and Zebul drove Gaal and his brothers out of Shechem.

units.label: The city destroyed and the tower burned
units.note: sown with salt, consumed with fire

verses.text: The next day the people of Shechem went out into the fields, and this was reported to Abimelech.
So he took his men, divided them into three companies, and lay in wait in the fields. When he saw the people coming out of the city, he rose up against them and attacked them.
Then Abimelech and the companies with him rushed forward and took their stand at the entrance of the city gate. The other two companies rushed against all who were in the fields and struck them down.
And all that day Abimelech fought against the city until he had captured it and killed its people. Then he demolished the city and sowed it with salt.
On hearing of this, all the leaders in the tower of Shechem entered the inner chamber of the temple of El-berith.
And when Abimelech was told that all the leaders in the tower of Shechem were gathered there,

he and all his men went up to Mount Zalmon. Abimelech took his axe in his hand and cut a branch from the trees, which he lifted to his shoulder, saying to his men, “Hurry and do what you have seen me do.”

So each man also cut his own branch and followed Abimelech. Then they piled the branches against the inner chamber and set it on fire above them, killing everyone in the tower of Shechem, about a thousand men and women.
Then Abimelech went to Thebez, encamped against it, and captured it.
But there was a strong tower inside the city, and all the men, women, and leaders of the city fled there. They locked themselves in and went up to the roof of the tower.
When Abimelech came to attack the tower, he approached its entrance to set it on fire.
But a woman dropped an upper millstone on Abimelech’s head, crushing his skull.
He quickly called his armor-bearer, saying, “Draw your sword and kill me, lest they say of me, ‘A woman killed him.’” So Abimelech’s armor-bearer ran his sword through him, and he died.
And when the Israelites saw that Abimelech was dead, they all went home.
In this way God repaid the wickedness that Abimelech had done to his father in murdering his seventy brothers.
And God also brought all the wickedness of the men of Shechem back upon their own heads. So the {{qelalah|curse}} of Jotham son of Jerubbaal came upon them.

abimelech.gloss: The name Abimelech means my father is king. Gideon had refused the crown yet named this son so, and the son now makes the name his ambition. The name hangs over the whole chapter, a claim to royalty that Abimelech pursues through fratricide rather than by God's appointment.

melek.gloss: King. Israel's true King was the LORD, and any human king was to be raised up by God and to rule under Him, Deuteronomy 17. Abimelech is made king, verse 6, not by the LORD but by the leaders of Shechem and with pagan silver; it is kingship seized for self, the very opposite of God's design and of the King to come.

atad.gloss: A thornbush or bramble, a worthless, prickly shrub giving no fruit and casting almost no shade, yet quick to catch fire and spread it. In Jotham's fable the fruitful trees decline the crown and only the bramble grasps at it, a biting picture of Abimelech, useless to shelter but ready to burn all who trust him.

qelalah.gloss: A curse, the invoking of ruin, the opposite of a blessing. Jotham pronounces a conditional curse that Abimelech and Shechem would consume one another with fire. The narrator closes the chapter by declaring that this very curse came upon them, showing God's just hand behind the unfolding disaster.

basic.context: Gideon refused to be king, but his son Abimelech craves the throne. Backed by his mother's clan at Shechem and paid from the temple of Baal-berith, he hires reckless men and murders his seventy brothers on a single stone; only the youngest, Jotham, escapes by hiding. Shechem crowns Abimelech king. Jotham then stands on Mount Gerizim and tells a fable: the trees looked for a king, and the useful olive, fig, and vine all declined, so only the worthless bramble accepted, offering false shade and threatening fire. Jotham warns that if their dealing with Gideon's house was treacherous, fire will break out between Abimelech and Shechem and consume them both, then he flees. Three years later God sends a spirit of animosity; Gaal leads a revolt; Abimelech destroys Shechem, sows it with salt, and burns a thousand in the tower. Finally, besieging Thebez, he is killed when a woman drops a millstone on his head, and God is said to have repaid his evil and fulfilled Jotham's curse.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative that describes, and clearly condemns, self-appointed kingship. The narrator does not hold up Abimelech as any kind of model; he is a cautionary figure whose reign begins in murder and ends in disgrace. Jotham's fable is the interpretive key God gives us: fruitful people serve, but only a bramble grasps for power it cannot bear. The recurring note that God sent the spirit, and God repaid the evil, teaches that the whole bloody unraveling is God's righteous justice, not blind fate.
basic.originalLanguages: The name Abimelech means my father is king, and he sets out to make the boast true. He is made melek, king, not by the LORD but by Shechem's leaders with pagan silver. Jotham likens him to the atad, the bramble, a worthless shrub that gives no shelter but readily burns. And the chapter ends by naming the qelalah, the curse, of Jotham fulfilled, as God repays evil upon both king and city.
basic.typology: Abimelech is the dark anti-type of the true King. He seizes the throne by murdering the sons of the deliverer, rules by force and fear, gives no true shelter, and destroys those he claims to lead, dying in shame. He is the exact negative image of Christ, the King who lays down His own life rather than take His brothers' lives, who gives real refuge, and who is exalted rather than disgraced. The bramble that only burns makes us long for the true Vine and King.
basic.christ: Everything Abimelech is, Christ is not. Abimelech grasped a kingship his father refused and secured it by shedding his brothers' blood; Christ, offered every kingdom, refused to grasp and secured His throne by shedding His own blood for His brothers. Abimelech was a bramble giving false shade and deadly fire; Christ is the true refuge under whose shadow sinners are safe. Abimelech's reign ended in a curse; Christ became a curse for us to redeem us, Galatians 3:13. The horror of self-made kingship here makes the beauty of the servant King shine all the brighter.
basic.god: Though the LORD is barely named in the human scheming, He governs the whole story. God sent the spirit of animosity that unraveled the conspiracy, God ensured the murdered brothers' blood was avenged, and God repaid the evil of both Abimelech and Shechem so that Jotham's curse came true. The chapter reveals a God who is patient but not mocked, who brings the wickedness of the violent back upon their own heads, and who will not let injustice stand forever, even when He seems, for a time, to be silent.
basic.crossReferences: Deuteronomy 17 sets God's design for a king, chosen by the LORD, which Abimelech utterly violates. Galatians 6 says a man reaps what he sows, exactly what befalls Abimelech and Shechem. Psalm 7 speaks of the violence that returns on the doer's own head. And Galatians 3 shows Christ becoming a curse for us, the reverse of the curse that consumes Abimelech.
basic.oneStory: Abimelech is the nightmare of kingship seized for self, and God repays it with the very ruin Jotham foretold. It stands in Judges as proof that a people who will not have the LORD as King, and the tyrants who exploit them, reap only fire. The whole grim episode intensifies the Bible's search for a righteous King, one who saves rather than slaughters, who shelters rather than burns, fulfilled at last in Jesus Christ.

context.literary: Chapter 9 is the longest single story of a single figure in Judges, and it is deliberately dark, a sustained account not of a deliverer but of a destroyer. It flows straight out of chapter 8: Gideon refused kingship and named a son Abimelech, my father is king, and now that son makes the name a program of murder. The chapter is framed by Jotham's fable and curse at the start and their fulfillment at the end, so the reader watches prophecy unfold as history. The theological refrains, God sent, God repaid, interpret the carnage as divine justice.
context.historical: Shechem was an old and significant city in central Canaan with a mixed population and a temple to Baal-berith, or El-berith, lord of the covenant. Abimelech's power base was his mother's Shechemite clan, and his kingship was a local, city-based affair, not a rule over all Israel in any settled sense. Killing rival heirs to secure a throne was a known practice in the ancient Near East. Sowing a destroyed city with salt was a symbolic act of utter desolation and cursing.
context.canonical: This is the anti-kingship narrative of Judges, the negative foil to the monarchy the book is quietly preparing for with its refrain that Israel had no king. Abimelech shows what happens when a man makes himself king apart from God, and it stands in the background when Israel later demands a king and when Saul's self-serving reign fails. Over against every Abimelech and Saul, Scripture points forward to the true King of David's line, and finally to Christ, who reigns in righteousness.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To expose the horror of kingship seized by ambition and blood, to show through Jotham's fable and its fulfillment that God repays such evil, and to deepen Israel's longing for a righteous king given by God rather than grasped by force.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes Abimelech's murders, Shechem's treachery, and the mutual destruction that follows; it prescribes none of it. What endures is the warning against grasping power for self, the certainty that violence recoils on the violent, and the assurance that God secretly and justly governs even the darkest human schemes.

debates.question: What is the point of Jotham's fable of the trees?

views.label: A satire on grasping for kingship
views.case: The fruitful trees, olive, fig, and vine, all decline the crown because they are busy giving good things that honor God and people; only the useless bramble, with nothing to offer, eagerly grasps it. The fable exposes Abimelech as a worthless man who craves rule precisely because he has no worth to give, and warns that such a king can only burn.

views.label: A warning against Shechem's folly and coming judgment
views.case: The sting is aimed at the leaders of Shechem who chose the bramble; the closing image of fire from the bramble consuming the cedars foretells the mutual destruction to come, so the fable functions less as character study and more as a prophetic threat that they have chosen their own ruin.

debates.bearing: Both readings converge: grasping for power is the mark of the worthless, and choosing such a ruler brings fire; the fable teaches that God's kind of king serves and gives, while the self-seeking king destroys.

debates.question: In what sense did God send a spirit of animosity between Abimelech and Shechem?

views.label: God's just governance over evil actors
views.case: Verse 24 states the purpose plainly: so that the murder of the seventy sons might be avenged; God sovereignly turned the wicked alliance against itself to bring justice, working through, not against, the free malice of guilty people.

views.label: God giving sinners over to their own hostility
views.case: The spirit of animosity, or harmful spirit, may describe God handing Abimelech and Shechem over to the very distrust and violence they had chosen, letting their sin become its own punishment, much as Romans 1 speaks of God giving people over to what they want.

debates.bearing: Either way God is shown to be the righteous ruler behind the ruin: He is not the author of their evil, but He justly directs it so that bloodshed recoils on the guilty, assuring us that no wickedness finally escapes His justice.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter's key words carry its message. The name Abimelech means my father is king, and the whole tragedy is this son trying, by any means, to make that name his own reality. He is made melek, king, verse 6, but not by the LORD, Israel's true King; he is crowned by the leaders of Shechem and financed with silver from the temple of Baal-berith, kingship purchased and seized rather than given by God. At the heart stands Jotham's fable, where the useful olive, fig, and vine all refuse the throne, and only the atad, the thornbush or bramble, accepts, a worthless shrub that offers no real shade but readily catches fire and spreads it, an exact portrait of Abimelech. Jotham then pronounces a qelalah, a curse, that fire would break out between Abimelech and Shechem and consume them both. The narrator ends by declaring that this qelalah came true and that God repaid the evil. The words trace a straight line from grasping ambition to divine justice: the man who would be king by blood becomes the bramble that burns, and the curse he earned falls on his own head.

typology.type: The false king who takes his brothers' lives to seize a throne
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the true King who lays down His own life for His brothers and gains His throne through self-sacrifice, John 10:11 and Hebrews 2:11-14.

typology.type: The bramble that gives no shelter but only fire
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the true refuge in whose shadow His people are kept safe, Isaiah 32:1-2 and Psalm 91:1-4.

typology.type: The reign that ends under a curse
typology.fulfillment: Christ, who became a curse for us on the tree so that we might be blessed, Galatians 3:13-14.

study.christ: Abimelech is the photographic negative of Jesus Christ, and the contrast preaches the gospel. Abimelech grasped a crown his father had refused and secured it by murdering his seventy brothers on one stone; Christ, though rightful King, did not grasp but humbled Himself, and won His throne by laying down His own life for His brothers, Philippians 2:6-8 and John 10:11. Abimelech was the bramble of Jotham's fable, giving no shelter and only fire; Christ is the true King in whose shadow the weary find refuge, Isaiah 32:1-2. Abimelech's reign began in blood and ended under a curse, crushed and disgraced; Christ willingly became a curse for us, hung on a tree, so that the blessing of God might come to us, Galatians 3:13-14. And where God repaid Abimelech's evil with just ruin, God raised Christ, who bore our evil, to unending life and an unshakable kingdom. The horror of the false king makes us long for, and love, the King who saves.
study.god: God is almost hidden in the first half of the chapter, absent from the plotting, the crowning, the fable spoken by a man; yet He is the true ruler of the whole account. It is God who sends the spirit of animosity that fractures the guilty alliance, God who ensures the blood of the seventy murdered brothers is avenged, and God who repays the wickedness of both Abimelech and the men of Shechem so that Jotham's curse comes to pass. The chapter reveals a God who is long-suffering but never mocked, who allows evil to run for a season, three years here, but then brings the violence of the violent crashing back upon their own heads. He is the God who governs even the schemes of tyrants and the treachery of cities, and who will not leave the murder of the innocent unanswered, a sober comfort to all who wait on His justice.

crossReferences.note: God's design that Israel's king be chosen by the LORD and rule under His law, everything Abimelech's self-made kingship violates.
Gideon refuses kingship and names his son Abimelech, my father is king, directly setting up the tragedy of chapter 9.
A man reaps what he sows, exactly fulfilled as Abimelech and Shechem reap the ruin they sowed.
The violence of the wicked returns upon their own head, the pattern the narrator names as God repaying the evil.
Christ became a curse for us on the tree, the gracious reverse of the curse that consumes Abimelech.

study.oneStory: Abimelech seizes by murder the crown his father declined, and God, honoring Jotham's curse, turns the conspirators against one another until Shechem and its self-made king consume each other in fire, and a woman's millstone ends the tyrant in disgrace. In Judges this is the portrait of kingship gone monstrous, proof that a people who will not have the LORD as King, and the tyrants who exploit them, reap only ruin. It sharpens the whole Bible's cry for a righteous King who shelters rather than burns and saves rather than slaughters, the cry answered in Jesus Christ, who won His throne not by taking His brothers' lives but by giving His own.

quiz.q: How did Abimelech become king at Shechem?

options.text: He won over his mother's clan, was funded from a pagan temple, and murdered his seventy brothers, so Shechem crowned him.
options.note: Verses 1-6 record the silver from Baal-berith, the murder, and the crowning.

options.text: He was chosen by the leaders of Shechem after proving himself in battle.
options.note: Shechem did crown him, but the path to it was fratricide and pagan money, not honorable service.

options.text: He inherited the throne from his father Gideon.
options.note: Gideon refused kingship and left no throne; Abimelech seized power by murder.

options.text: God appointed him king over all Israel.
options.note: God did not appoint him; he was made king by Shechem, against God's design.

quiz.q: What is the point of Jotham's fable of the trees?

options.text: The fruitful trees decline the crown while only the worthless bramble grasps it, exposing Abimelech as a useless man who can only burn those he rules.
options.note: Verses 8-15 satirize grasping ambition and warn of coming fire.

options.text: It teaches that trees, like people, need a strong ruler to survive.
options.note: It touches on rule, but its point is the opposite: the worthy decline power and only the bramble grasps it.

options.text: It shows that the olive, fig, and vine were too proud to serve.
options.note: They declined not from pride but because they were busy giving good fruit; the bramble is the villain, not they.

options.text: It predicts a bountiful harvest for Shechem.
options.note: It predicts fire and mutual destruction, not blessing, for Shechem.

quiz.q: Why did God send a spirit of animosity between Abimelech and the leaders of Shechem?

options.text: So that the murder of Gideon's seventy sons might come to justice and their blood be avenged on both.
options.note: Verse 24 states this explicit purpose of divine justice.

options.text: Because Abimelech had failed to defend the city well.
options.note: There was conflict over rule, but the stated reason is justice for the murdered brothers, not military failure.

options.text: To give Gaal a chance to become king instead.
options.note: Gaal's revolt is part of the unraveling, but God's stated aim was justice, not promoting Gaal.

options.text: For no reason the text explains.
options.note: The text plainly gives the reason: to avenge the seventy sons.

options.text: A woman dropped an upper millstone on his head, and he had his armor-bearer finish him off.
options.note: Verses 53-54 record the millstone and the shameful death he tried to hide.

options.text: He was killed in open battle against Gaal.
options.note: He defeated Gaal earlier; his death came later at Thebez, by a millstone, not against Gaal.

options.text: He burned to death in the tower he attacked.
options.note: He burned others in the tower of Shechem; he himself died from a millstone at Thebez.

options.text: He reigned long and died peacefully.
options.note: His reign was short and violent, ending in a disgraceful death, not peace.

quiz.q: How does the narrator explain the disasters that ended Abimelech and Shechem?

options.text: God repaid the wickedness of both, so that Jotham's curse came upon them.
options.note: Verses 56-57 name God's just repayment and the fulfillment of the curse.

options.text: It was simply the fortunes of war.
options.note: There was warfare, but the narrator traces the outcome to God's justice, not mere chance.

options.text: The people of Shechem repented and were spared.
options.note: Shechem was destroyed, not spared; there is no repentance in the account.

options.text: Abimelech's death had nothing to do with his crimes.
options.note: The text explicitly links his death to God repaying his murder of his brothers.

quiz.q: How does Abimelech's story point us to Christ?

options.text: As the false king who took his brothers' lives to grasp a throne, he is the dark opposite of Christ, who gave His own life to win His throne for His brothers.
options.note: The contrast magnifies Christ, the servant King who saves rather than slaughters.

options.text: He shows that a strong ruler must eliminate all rivals.
options.note: The chapter condemns exactly this; Christ's kingship is the opposite of eliminating rivals by blood.

options.text: He proves that God does not care how kings gain power.
options.note: God plainly judged how Abimelech gained power, repaying his evil.

options.text: He has no connection to Christ at all.
options.note: By stark contrast he points to Christ, the true and righteous King.

questions: Abimelech grasped a throne by shedding his brothers' blood, while Christ won His throne by shedding His own. Where are you tempted to grasp for what you want rather than to serve and give?

In Jotham's fable the fruitful trees were too busy giving good things to crave power, and only the empty bramble grasped it. What does this say about the difference between ambition and fruitfulness in your own life?
The bramble offered false shade but real fire. Who or what are you tempted to take refuge in that cannot truly shelter you, and how does Christ offer better refuge?
God worked justice slowly, over three years, but surely. How does the certainty that God repays evil steady you when you see the violent and dishonest prospering for a time?
Abimelech is everything Christ is not, a king who destroys rather than saves. How does gazing at the servant King who gave His life for His brothers move your worship this week?

translationNotes.note: Jerubbaal is another name for Gideon, meaning let Baal contend, from Judges 6:32; the name recurs throughout this chapter, keeping the murdered father's household in view.
Spirit of animosity renders a phrase the BSB footnote also gives as a harmful spirit; the text stresses that God sovereignly sent it to bring the murderers to justice, verse 24.
The BSB follows the Septuagint in verse 29; the Hebrew reads slightly differently, with Gaal's challenge to Abimelech phrased as a taunt to muster his army and come out.
El-berith, god of the covenant, is another name for Baal-berith of verse 4; the leaders sought refuge in the very pagan temple that had funded Abimelech's rise, and it became their tomb.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Judges 9:1-57, including the poetic fable of verses 7-15. Em dashes in the source at verses 16 and 18 were converted to commas; no em or en dashes remain anywhere in the file. Sermon set to none; no verified sermon from the trusted cluster confirmed this session for this passage.
